mainEntity.name: Why does my daughter runs. not away from me — toward something only she can see. a reflection in a window. a sound in the distance. a thought in her head that pulls her like a magnet. one moment she's beside me, the next she's gone. no warning. no hesitation. no looking back. she doesn't understand danger. she doesn't know that cars don't stop, that water is deep, that strangers aren't always safe. she just runs — fast, silent, determined — and my heart stops every single time. i've chased her through parking lots, dodging cars while screaming her name. i've sprinted across busy streets, my body between her and traffic. i've tackled her on sidewalks while people stared and judged. i've searched stores in panic mode, checking under every rack, behind every display. i've lost her in crowds and felt terror so complete i couldn't breathe, couldn't think, couldn't do anything but run and scream and pray. she's four years old and i cannot take my eyes off her for three seconds. three seconds is all it takes. i time it now — i know exactly how fast she can disappear. she doesn't sleep through the night, and neither do i, because she's learned to unlock doors. she figured out the chain lock. she moved furniture to reach the deadbolt. i installed locks she can't reach, and she found ways around them. i sleep in front of her door some nights because it's the only way to know she won't get out. every outing is a risk assessment. every public space is a potential disaster. i scan for exits before we enter anywhere. i position myself between her and the door. i hold her hand so tight it probably hurts, but if i loosen my grip for one second — to pay for something, to help her brother, to answer a question — she's gone. she doesn't respond to her name when she's running. she doesn't stop when i call. it's like she can't hear me, or the pull toward wherever she's going is stronger than any voice. i've tried reasoning with her. i've tried consequences. i've tried social stories and rewards and everything anyone has suggested. but she doesn't learn the way other children learn. danger doesn't register. the connection between running and getting hurt doesn't form. she runs today exactly like she ran a year ago, two years ago. other parents don't understand. they think i'm overprotective. they think she'll grow out of it. they let their children run ahead at the park while i grip my daughter like she might evaporate. they don't see that my child would run straight into traffic without slowing down. they don't know that she's been pulled from water twice. they don't understand that this isn't a discipline problem or a parenting failure — it's her brain, her neurology, her complete inability to perceive danger the way other children do. i need help. not advice about being consistent. not judgment about my hovering. help. i need tools that keep her safe when my body can't be everywhere at once. i need systems that work when i'm exhausted, when i'm distracted for a moment, when i'm human and imperfect. i need alarms on every door and locks she can't defeat. i need a way to find her if the worst happens. i need identification on her body because she can't tell strangers who she is. i need the world to understand that keeping this child alive is a full-time job that never ends, and i'm terrified every single day that one day i'll fail. one moment of inattention. one lock she figures out. one crowd too dense. one second too slow. that's all it would take. and i can't let that happen. i won't. so i need every tool, every strategy, every material that exists. because her safety isn't negotiable, and right now, i'm fighting for her life with nothing but my own exhausted vigilance?

acceptedAnswer.text: ELOPEMENT / BOLTING BEHAVIOR / WANDERING / FLIGHT RESPONSE / SAFETY-SEEKING OR STIMULUS-SEEKING LOCOMOTION! Elopement (also called bolting, wandering, or running) refers to a child leaving a supervised, safe area without permission or awareness of danger. This behavior is particularly prevalent in children with autism spectrum disorder (studies suggest nearly 50% of children with ASD elope at some point), but also occurs in children with ADHD, intellectual disabilities, anxiety disorders, trauma histories, and sensory processing differences. Elopement is a safety-critical behavior — it is associated with drowning (the leading cause of death for children with autism who wander), traffic accidents, exposure, and other life-threatening outcomes. Elopement may be driven by various factors: sensory seeking (pursuing an attractive stimulus), escape from overwhelming environments, goal-directed behavior (wanting to reach a specific place or thing), impulsivity without danger awareness, or anxiety-driven flight. Unlike typical childhood running, elopement in these populations often occurs without danger awareness, without response to calls or commands, and with intensity and persistence that makes interception difficult. Management requires multi-layered approaches: environmental controls (preventing departure), tracking systems (locating if departure occurs), identification (enabling return if found), and therapeutic intervention (addressing underlying drivers while building safety awareness).

mainEntity.name: What is Elopement / Bolting Behavior / Wandering / Flight Response / Safety-Seeking or Stimulus-Seeking Locomotion?

acceptedAnswer.text: Elopement (also called bolting, wandering, or running) refers to a child leaving a supervised, safe area without permission or awareness of danger. This behavior is particularly prevalent in children with autism spectrum disorder (studies suggest nearly 50% of children with ASD elope at some point), but also occurs in children with ADHD, intellectual disabilities, anxiety disorders, trauma histories, and sensory processing differences. Elopement is a safety-critical behavior — it is associated with drowning (the leading cause of death for children with autism who wander), traffic accidents, exposure, and other life-threatening outcomes. Elopement may be driven by various factors: sensory seeking (pursuing an attractive stimulus), escape from overwhelming environments, goal-directed behavior (wanting to reach a specific place or thing), impulsivity without danger awareness, or anxiety-driven flight. Unlike typical childhood running, elopement in these populations often occurs without danger awareness, without response to calls or commands, and with intensity and persistence that makes interception difficult. Management requires multi-layered approaches: environmental controls (preventing departure), tracking systems (locating if departure occurs), identification (enabling return if found), and therapeutic intervention (addressing underlying drivers while building safety awareness). Common signs include: Sudden bolting without warning or apparent trigger, Running toward specific attractors (water, vehicles, sounds, lights), Running from overwhelming stimuli (noise, crowds, demands), No response to name or commands when running, No awareness of dangers (traffic, water, heights, strangers).

mainEntity.name: How does a Door and Window Alarms help with elopement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Door and window alarms are the first line of defense against elopement from home. These devices alert caregivers immediately when a door or window is opened, providing critical seconds to intervene before a child can get far. For children who elope during nighttime hours or when caregivers are briefly occupied, alarms transform a silent escape into an announced one. Alarms should be loud enough to wake sleeping caregivers and positioned where the child cannot easily disable them. Multiple layers may be needed — alarms on bedroom doors, exterior doors, and windows — depending on the child's escape patterns. The goal is not to trap the child but to ensure no exit goes unnoticed, giving caregivers the chance to respond before the child reaches danger. Some systems can be connected to smartphones for remote alerts, useful when multiple caregivers share responsibility. Price: ₹500–3,000. DIY option: Basic door alarms are inexpensive and battery-operated. Mount high enough that child can't disable. Consider alarms on interior doors (bedroom, bathroom) to detect nighttime movement. Test regularly. Have backup batteries always available. Safety: Alarms are notification, not prevention. They tell you the child is leaving but don't stop them. Alarms must be combined with other measures (locks, supervision) for children who can exit faster than caregivers can respond.

mainEntity.name: How does a Child-Resistant Door Locks and Latches help with elopement?

acceptedAnswer.text: While alarms notify, locks prevent. Child-resistant locks and latches create physical barriers between the child and exits. Standard locks are often defeated by determined children — they learn to reach deadbolts, figure out chain locks, and move furniture to access higher mechanisms. Specialized child-resistant locks require fine motor skills, sequences, or strengths that young children typically lack. Options include locks positioned at adult height, double-keyed deadbolts (requiring keys on both sides), coded locks, and specialized latches designed to resist child manipulation. The key is layering: a child who defeats one lock encounters another. Window locks, sliding door locks, and gate latches require equal attention — children will find the weakest point. Balance is essential: locks must prevent child exit while still allowing adult exit in emergencies and meeting fire safety codes. Price: ₹300–2,500. DIY option: Add secondary locks at adult height. Use door knob covers that require grip strength children lack. Consider double-keyed deadbolts but keep keys accessible for emergency exit. Secure sliding doors with bars or pins. Lock windows with specialized latches. Regularly test all locks as child's abilities develop. Safety: Fire safety is critical. All adults must be able to exit quickly in emergencies. Keep keys for double-keyed locks in accessible but consistent locations known to all adults. Check local fire codes regarding lock types.

mainEntity.name: How does a GPS Tracking Devices help with elopement?

acceptedAnswer.text: When prevention fails, location becomes critical. GPS tracking devices allow caregivers to locate a child who has eloped, potentially reducing search time from hours to minutes. Seconds matter when a child is heading toward water or traffic. GPS devices come in various forms: watches, clip-on trackers, shoe inserts, and belt attachments. The best device is the one the child will keep on — some children remove bracelets or dislike watches, requiring alternative placements. Key features include real-time location tracking, geofencing alerts (notification when child leaves a designated safe zone), and long battery life. Caregivers should have the tracking app on multiple phones and test the system regularly. GPS provides peace of mind and, critically, provides first responders with a location when every second counts. These devices don't prevent elopement, but they dramatically improve outcomes when it occurs. Price: ₹2,000–15,000. DIY option: Choose devices based on what child will tolerate wearing. Test location accuracy in your area. Set geofence alerts for home, school, and other regular locations. Ensure multiple family members have access to tracking app. Keep device charged — build charging into daily routine. Have backup plan if device fails. Safety: GPS is location, not prevention. Don't rely on GPS as primary safety measure — it helps you find them after they've left, not stop them from leaving. Battery failure is always possible; have backup identification.

mainEntity.name: How does a Medical ID and Safety Identification help with elopement?

acceptedAnswer.text: When a child who elopes is found by strangers, they often cannot provide their name, address, or parent contact information. Children with autism or intellectual disabilities may not respond to questions or may respond in ways that confuse finders. Medical ID and safety identification worn on the body provides critical information to anyone who finds the child: name, diagnosis, emergency contact numbers, communication challenges, and any medical information responders need. Options include engraved bracelets and necklaces, temporary tattoos with parent phone numbers, ID cards attached to clothing, shoe tags, and iron-on labels. The key is permanence — identification that can't easily be removed by the child and that will be noticed by finders. Some families use multiple forms: bracelet plus shoe tag plus temporary tattoo. Identification should include at minimum: child's name, parent phone number, notation of any communication or safety challenges, and allergies or medical conditions. Price: ₹200–2,000. DIY option: Order medical ID bracelet with key information engraved. Add temporary safety tattoos with phone number (reapply regularly). Attach ID tags to shoelaces or belt loops. Iron identification labels inside clothing. Include diagnosis, communication notes, and emergency contact on all ID. Take current photos regularly for search purposes. Safety: Some children will remove ID. Use multiple forms in multiple locations. Check regularly that ID is still present and information is current. Update contact numbers if they change.

mainEntity.name: How does a Fencing and Perimeter Security help with elopement?

acceptedAnswer.text: For children who elope from yards or outdoor spaces, fencing and perimeter security create physical containment that allows outdoor access without escape risk. Standard fencing may be inadequate for determined climbers or children who find gaps. Effective perimeter security considers height (tall enough to prevent climbing), material (difficult to grip or climb), gate security (locks the child cannot defeat), and sight lines (can caregivers see all areas from supervision points). Pool fencing is particularly critical — drowning is the leading cause of death for children with autism who wander. Pool fences should have self-closing, self-latching gates with child-resistant locks. Perimeter security allows children some freedom of outdoor movement while maintaining safety boundaries. For families without the ability to fully fence, smaller secured play areas within the yard may provide safe outdoor space. Price: ₹10,000–100,000+. DIY option: Audit current fencing for gaps, climb points, and gate security. Add height extensions if needed. Install child-resistant gate latches. Ensure pool is separately fenced with self-closing gate. Remove objects near fence that could serve as climbing aids. Consider motion sensors for perimeter alerts. Safety: Fencing must be paired with water safety: pools, ponds, streams, and any water body on or near property must be inaccessible. Children who elope are often drawn to water.

mainEntity.name: How does a Safety Harnesses and Wrist Links help with elopement?

acceptedAnswer.text: In public spaces where environmental controls aren't possible, safety harnesses and wrist links provide physical connection between child and caregiver. For children who bolt without warning, a wrist link or harness can mean the difference between a contained situation and a life-threatening chase. These tools are controversial — some view them as stigmatizing — but for families of children who elope, they can be lifesaving. Options range from simple wrist-to-wrist links to backpack harnesses with handles. The key is fit: comfortable enough for the child to tolerate, secure enough that they can't escape, and appropriate for the environment. Harnesses work best in high-risk settings: parking lots, near traffic, in crowds, near water. They supplement supervision; they don't replace it. Some families use harnesses during transition periods while working on skills that will eventually make them unnecessary. Price: ₹500–2,500. DIY option: Choose harness or wrist link based on child's tolerance and escape abilities. Test before using in high-risk settings. Practice using at home so child acclimates. Keep backup hand-holding as well — harness supplements, doesn't replace. Have response ready for public comments. Safety: Harnesses require caregiver strength to be effective. They prevent bolting but don't prevent pulling — be prepared to brace. Never attach to fixed objects; always to adult body. Check for wear and secure fastening regularly.

@graph.name: How to Help a Child with Elopement
@graph.description: A graded approach using evidence-based materials. Elopement is a safety-critical behavior that requires layered management: prevention (stopping departure), tracking (locating if departure occurs), identification (enabling return if found), and therapeutic intervention (addressing underlying drivers while building safety awareness). These layers work together — no single approach is sufficient for severe elopement. Materials keep children alive while therapy builds the skills that may eventually make some materials unnecessary. Safety first, always. The goal is a living child who can continue to develop, learn, and grow.

Elopement is a safety-critical behavior — it is associated with drowning (the leading cause of death for children with autism who wander), traffic accidents, exposure, and other life-threatening outcomes. Elopement may be driven by various factors: sensory seeking (pursuing an attractive stimulus), escape from overwhelming environments, goal-directed behavior (wanting to reach a specific place or thing), impulsivity without danger awareness, or anxiety-driven flight. Unlike typical childhood running, elopement in these populations often occurs without danger awareness, without response to calls or commands, and with intensity and persistence that makes interception difficult. Management requires multi-layered approaches: environmental controls (preventing departure), tracking systems (locating if departure occurs), identification (enabling return if found), and therapeutic intervention (addressing underlying drivers while building safety awareness). citation.name: Research on Autism Elopement and Wandering
citation.description: Research indicates that nearly half (49%) of children with autism spectrum disorder attempt to elope, with a significant percentage going missing long enough to cause concern. Drowning is the leading cause of death for children with autism who wander, accounting for approximately 90% of lethal outcomes. Elopement is associated with communication challenges, impulse control differences, reduced danger awareness, and sensory-seeking behavior. Multi-layered safety approaches combining environmental controls, tracking technology, identification, and therapeutic intervention are recommended.

@graph.name: 9 Materials for Elopement

item.name: Door and Window Alarms
item.description: Door and window alarms are the first line of defense against elopement from home. These devices alert caregivers immediately when a door or window is opened, providing critical seconds to intervene before a child can get far. For children who elope during nighttime hours or when caregivers are briefly occupied, alarms transform a silent escape into an announced one. Alarms should be loud enough to wake sleeping caregivers and positioned where the child cannot easily disable them. Multiple layers may be needed — alarms on bedroom doors, exterior doors, and windows — depending on the child's escape patterns. The goal is not to trap the child but to ensure no exit goes unnoticed, giving caregivers the chance to respond before the child reaches danger. Some systems can be connected to smartphones for remote alerts, useful when multiple caregivers share responsibility.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Basic door alarms are inexpensive and battery-operated. Mount high enough that child can't disable. Consider alarms on interior doors (bedroom, bathroom) to detect nighttime movement. Test regularly. Have backup batteries always available.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Alarms are notification, not prevention. They tell you the child is leaving but don't stop them. Alarms must be combined with other measures (locks, supervision) for children who can exit faster than caregivers can respond.

item.name: Child-Resistant Door Locks and Latches
item.description: While alarms notify, locks prevent. Child-resistant locks and latches create physical barriers between the child and exits. Standard locks are often defeated by determined children — they learn to reach deadbolts, figure out chain locks, and move furniture to access higher mechanisms. Specialized child-resistant locks require fine motor skills, sequences, or strengths that young children typically lack. Options include locks positioned at adult height, double-keyed deadbolts (requiring keys on both sides), coded locks, and specialized latches designed to resist child manipulation. The key is layering: a child who defeats one lock encounters another. Window locks, sliding door locks, and gate latches require equal attention — children will find the weakest point. Balance is essential: locks must prevent child exit while still allowing adult exit in emergencies and meeting fire safety codes.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Add secondary locks at adult height. Use door knob covers that require grip strength children lack. Consider double-keyed deadbolts but keep keys accessible for emergency exit. Secure sliding doors with bars or pins. Lock windows with specialized latches. Regularly test all locks as child's abilities develop.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Fire safety is critical. All adults must be able to exit quickly in emergencies. Keep keys for double-keyed locks in accessible but consistent locations known to all adults. Check local fire codes regarding lock types.

item.name: GPS Tracking Devices
item.description: When prevention fails, location becomes critical. GPS tracking devices allow caregivers to locate a child who has eloped, potentially reducing search time from hours to minutes. Seconds matter when a child is heading toward water or traffic. GPS devices come in various forms: watches, clip-on trackers, shoe inserts, and belt attachments. The best device is the one the child will keep on — some children remove bracelets or dislike watches, requiring alternative placements. Key features include real-time location tracking, geofencing alerts (notification when child leaves a designated safe zone), and long battery life. Caregivers should have the tracking app on multiple phones and test the system regularly. GPS provides peace of mind and, critically, provides first responders with a location when every second counts. These devices don't prevent elopement, but they dramatically improve outcomes when it occurs.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Choose devices based on what child will tolerate wearing. Test location accuracy in your area. Set geofence alerts for home, school, and other regular locations. Ensure multiple family members have access to tracking app. Keep device charged — build charging into daily routine. Have backup plan if device fails.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: GPS is location, not prevention. Don't rely on GPS as primary safety measure — it helps you find them after they've left, not stop them from leaving. Battery failure is always possible; have backup identification.

item.name: Medical ID and Safety Identification
item.description: When a child who elopes is found by strangers, they often cannot provide their name, address, or parent contact information. Children with autism or intellectual disabilities may not respond to questions or may respond in ways that confuse finders. Medical ID and safety identification worn on the body provides critical information to anyone who finds the child: name, diagnosis, emergency contact numbers, communication challenges, and any medical information responders need. Options include engraved bracelets and necklaces, temporary tattoos with parent phone numbers, ID cards attached to clothing, shoe tags, and iron-on labels. The key is permanence — identification that can't easily be removed by the child and that will be noticed by finders. Some families use multiple forms: bracelet plus shoe tag plus temporary tattoo. Identification should include at minimum: child's name, parent phone number, notation of any communication or safety challenges, and allergies or medical conditions.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Order medical ID bracelet with key information engraved. Add temporary safety tattoos with phone number (reapply regularly). Attach ID tags to shoelaces or belt loops. Iron identification labels inside clothing. Include diagnosis, communication notes, and emergency contact on all ID. Take current photos regularly for search purposes.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Some children will remove ID. Use multiple forms in multiple locations. Check regularly that ID is still present and information is current. Update contact numbers if they change.

item.name: Fencing and Perimeter Security
item.description: For children who elope from yards or outdoor spaces, fencing and perimeter security create physical containment that allows outdoor access without escape risk. Standard fencing may be inadequate for determined climbers or children who find gaps. Effective perimeter security considers height (tall enough to prevent climbing), material (difficult to grip or climb), gate security (locks the child cannot defeat), and sight lines (can caregivers see all areas from supervision points). Pool fencing is particularly critical — drowning is the leading cause of death for children with autism who wander. Pool fences should have self-closing, self-latching gates with child-resistant locks. Perimeter security allows children some freedom of outdoor movement while maintaining safety boundaries. For families without the ability to fully fence, smaller secured play areas within the yard may provide safe outdoor space.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Audit current fencing for gaps, climb points, and gate security. Add height extensions if needed. Install child-resistant gate latches. Ensure pool is separately fenced with self-closing gate. Remove objects near fence that could serve as climbing aids. Consider motion sensors for perimeter alerts.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Fencing must be paired with water safety: pools, ponds, streams, and any water body on or near property must be inaccessible. Children who elope are often drawn to water.

item.name: Safety Harnesses and Wrist Links
item.description: In public spaces where environmental controls aren't possible, safety harnesses and wrist links provide physical connection between child and caregiver. For children who bolt without warning, a wrist link or harness can mean the difference between a contained situation and a life-threatening chase. These tools are controversial — some view them as stigmatizing — but for families of children who elope, they can be lifesaving. Options range from simple wrist-to-wrist links to backpack harnesses with handles. The key is fit: comfortable enough for the child to tolerate, secure enough that they can't escape, and appropriate for the environment. Harnesses work best in high-risk settings: parking lots, near traffic, in crowds, near water. They supplement supervision; they don't replace it. Some families use harnesses during transition periods while working on skills that will eventually make them unnecessary.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Choose harness or wrist link based on child's tolerance and escape abilities. Test before using in high-risk settings. Practice using at home so child acclimates. Keep backup hand-holding as well — harness supplements, doesn't replace. Have response ready for public comments.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Harnesses require caregiver strength to be effective. They prevent bolting but don't prevent pulling — be prepared to brace. Never attach to fixed objects; always to adult body. Check for wear and secure fastening regularly.

item.name: Visual Boundary and Safety Markers
item.description: Some children can learn to recognize and respond to visual boundaries even when they don't understand abstract concepts of danger. Visual boundary markers make invisible rules visible: red tape across doorways means 'stop,' colored zones indicate safe and off-limits areas, visual barriers signal boundaries even when physical barriers aren't possible. These tools work best for children who are visual learners and who can be taught to respond to consistent visual cues. Boundary markers require teaching — the marker itself doesn't prevent crossing, but repeated practice with consistent reinforcement can build stopping responses. Visual boundaries are intermediate tools: more effective than verbal rules alone, less effective than physical barriers. They work best combined with other measures and for children whose elopement is impulsive rather than intensely goal-directed.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Use consistent visual markers: red tape for stop, specific colors for zones. Teach meaning explicitly and practice repeatedly. Reinforce stopping behavior heavily. Start with low-stakes boundaries before critical ones. Combine with other safety measures — visual boundaries are training tools, not reliable barriers.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Visual boundaries are teaching tools, not safety barriers. They may reduce casual boundary crossing but will likely not stop determined elopement. Always pair with physical safety measures for high-risk situations.

item.name: Sensory and Calming Tools for Elopement Triggers
item.description: Much elopement is function-based — children run toward attractors or away from aversives. Sensory and calming tools address the underlying drives that trigger elopement. If a child runs to escape overwhelming noise, noise-canceling headphones may prevent the flight trigger. If a child runs seeking movement input, heavy work activities or movement breaks may satisfy the need safely. If a child runs from anxiety or demand, calming tools and environmental modifications may reduce the drive to flee. This approach addresses root causes rather than just containment. Functional behavior assessment can identify specific elopement triggers; sensory tools then address those triggers. This doesn't replace physical safety measures but may reduce elopement frequency over time by meeting the needs the child was running to meet or escape from.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Identify elopement triggers through observation. For sensory overwhelm: provide escape via headphones, sunglasses, or retreat space. For movement-seeking: build in heavy work and movement breaks. For demand escape: modify demands and provide breaks. For specific attractors: provide safe access when possible. Work with therapists on functional behavior assessment.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Addressing triggers is valuable but not sufficient for severe elopement. Physical safety measures remain essential while therapeutic intervention proceeds. Some children's elopement is not easily traced to single triggers.

item.name: Water Safety Equipment and Pool Alarms
item.description: Drowning is the leading cause of death for children with autism who wander. Many children who elope are specifically drawn to water — pools, ponds, streams, lakes. Water safety equipment is not optional for families of children who elope; it is essential. Pool alarms detect entry into water and sound alerts. Pool covers provide physical barriers. Pool fencing with self-closing, self-latching gates separates water from access. Life jackets worn near any water body provide protection if a child reaches water before interception. Beyond home pools, families must consider community water hazards: neighbors' pools, retention ponds, streams, and other water sources in elopement range. Water safety requires absolute vigilance and multiple layers: fence the pool, alarm the pool, lock the gates, know where all water is located within your child's potential travel range.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Audit all water sources within potential elopement range — home, neighbors, community. Fence pools with self-closing, self-latching gates. Install pool alarms. Use pool covers when pool is unattended. Have child wear life jacket near any water. Consider swimming lessons for water survival skills. Alert neighbors to your child's elopement risk and water attraction.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: No single water safety measure is sufficient. Layers are essential: fencing AND alarms AND covers AND supervision AND life jackets when near water. Drowning can occur in minutes and can be silent.

socialMedia.caption: When your child runs without warning, without fear, without stopping — every second counts. Elopement (bolting/wandering) is a safety crisis for many families. Here are 9 materials that create layers of protection: door alarms, child-resistant locks, GPS trackers, medical ID, secure fencing, safety harnesses, visual boundaries, sensory tools addressing triggers, and water safety equipment. Prevention. Tracking. Identification. Because your vigilance needs backup. Save to build your safety system! 🔖 #ElopementPrevention #AutismSafety #PinnacleBlooms
